## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEAUT635]**

#### **Q1. List any three common causes of a "soft brake pedal" in a hydraulic brake system and state the corrective action for each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Air trapped in hydraulic lines** — The pedal feels spongy because air is compressible. Corrective action: bleed the brake system in the prescribed sequence (right-rear → left-rear → right-front → left-front for a diagonal-split system).
2. **Internal leak in the master cylinder (bypassing seals)** — Brake fluid leaks past the primary or secondary cup seal back into the reservoir, so pressure cannot be sustained. Corrective action: rebuild or replace the master cylinder and bleed the system.
3. **External fluid leak** — at a flexible hose, brake line, caliper, or wheel cylinder. Corrective action: identify the leak point by visual inspection and pressure-test, then replace the faulty component and bleed the system.

---

#### **Q2. What is the significance of the lateral runout limit (0.05 mm) for a brake disc? What symptom appears if this limit is exceeded?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **lateral runout limit of 0.05 mm** (per most OEM service manuals and ECE R13-H guidelines) is the maximum permissible deviation of the disc's friction surface from a true plane of rotation. If exceeded, the brake pad is forced to traverse this peak-and-valley geometry each revolution, producing a **cyclic force variation** that the driver feels as a **pulsating brake pedal and steering-wheel vibration under braking**. The corrective action is to either **resurface the disc on an on-car brake lathe** (only if the post-resurfacing thickness remains above the cast "MIN TH." marking) or to **replace the disc**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model, PEAUT635 — Module 2]**

#### **Question A**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch (block diagram) the working of a **tandem master cylinder** used in modern hydraulic brake systems. How does it provide a redundant failure mode? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vehicle of mass **1 200 kg** is travelling at **80 km/h** on a dry level road. The driver applies the brakes with a reaction time of **1.2 s**. The road–tyre friction coefficient is **$\mu = 0.8$**.
   Compute: (i) the deceleration, (ii) the braking-only stopping distance, and (iii) the total stopping distance. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

A **tandem master cylinder (TMC)** contains **two pistons in series** within a single bore, fed from a common reservoir with **two separate chambers**. The bore is divided into two **independent hydraulic circuits** (typically a diagonal split: LF+RR in one circuit, RF+LR in the other).

**Working principle:**
- When the brake pedal is depressed, the **primary piston** advances, building pressure in the **primary (rear) circuit**.
- The **secondary piston** is pushed by hydraulic pressure transmitted through a **centre valve** (or, in centre-valve-less designs, by direct mechanical contact via a spring-loaded intermediate seal).
- Pressure is simultaneously built in the **secondary (front) circuit**.
- Both circuits deliver fluid to their respective wheel brakes (front and rear, in a diagonal arrangement).

**Failure mode — safety redundancy:**
- If the **primary circuit leaks**, the primary piston travels its full stroke without building pressure. The secondary piston is then driven mechanically by the primary piston's tip through the intermediate spring, and the **secondary circuit still operates** — providing braking on two wheels (the diagonal pair).
- If the **secondary circuit leaks**, the primary circuit builds pressure normally and the **primary circuit still operates** — again providing braking on the opposite diagonal pair.
- In either single-circuit failure, the driver experiences a **longer pedal travel** and **reduced braking effort**, but **emergency stopping capability is retained**.

A small **low-pressure warning switch** is integrated into the TMC body — when pressure in one circuit drops below the threshold, the switch closes the brake-warning lamp circuit on the dashboard, alerting the driver.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Given:**
- $m = 1200$ kg (mass is not directly required for $\mu g$ model, but supplied for context).
- $v_0 = 80$ km/h.
- $t_r = 1.2$ s.
- $\mu = 0.8$.

**Step 1 — Unit conversion:**
$$v_0 = 80 \times \dfrac{1000}{3600} = 22.22 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Deceleration:**
$$a = \mu g = 0.8 \times 9.81 = 7.848 \ \text{m/s}^2 \approx 7.85 \ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Braking-only stopping distance:**
$$d_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g} = \dfrac{(22.22)^2}{2 \times 7.848} = \dfrac{493.7}{15.696} \approx 31.45 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Reaction distance:**
$$d_{\text{react}} = v_0 \cdot t_r = 22.22 \times 1.2 = 26.67 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Total stopping distance:**
$$d_{\text{total}} = d_{\text{react}} + d_{\text{brake}} = 26.67 + 31.45 = 58.12 \ \text{m} \approx 58.1 \ \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $a \approx 7.85 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- (ii) $d_{\text{brake}} \approx 31.45 \ \text{m}$
- (iii) $d_{\text{total}} \approx 58.1 \ \text{m}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common mistake here is omitting the **unit-conversion step** ($80 \text{ km/h} \to 22.22 \text{ m/s}$). A second common error is forgetting the **reaction-time term** when asked for "total" stopping distance — examiners specifically look for both $d_{\text{react}}$ and $d_{\text{brake}}$ computed separately. Finally, mass is *not* required in the $\mu g$ model and using it incorrectly (e.g., $F = ma$ then $a = F/m$ cancelling out) costs a mark for "not recognising the simplified model."

---

### 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEAUT635 — Module 2 Alternative Choice]**

#### **Question B**

**(a)** Discuss the **common brake issues** encountered in service workshops, with at least **six distinct symptoms**, their probable causes, and remedies. Present your answer in a structured tabular form. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**. List any **three common ABS faults** and the diagnostic steps for each. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

| # | Symptom | Top Causes | Diagnostic Test | Remedy |
|---|---------|------------|-----------------|--------|
| 1 | **Squealing / high-pitched noise** | (i) Pad wear-indicator touching rotor (ii) Glazed pad surface (iii) Missing anti-rattle shim | Visual inspection of pads and rotor edge | Replace pads; deglaze or replace; refit shim |
| 2 | **Grinding noise (metal-to-metal)** | Pad worn down to backing plate; debris between pad and caliper | Visual — see scoring marks on rotor | Replace pads and inspect/replace rotor |
| 3 | **Soft / spongy pedal** | Air in hydraulic lines; MC internal leak; external leak | Pump test; visual leak check; pressure-test MC | Bleed system; replace MC; repair leak |
| 4 | **Hard pedal (no boost)** | Vacuum hose leak; booster diaphragm rupture; check-valve stuck | Listen for hiss; vacuum-gauge test | Replace hose; replace booster; replace check-valve |
| 5 | **Pedal pulsation / vibration** | Warped disc (> 0.05 mm runout); out-of-round drum | DTI gauge on rotor face; runout of drum | Resurface or replace disc; replace drum |
| 6 | **Pulling to one side** | Seized caliper slide-pin; collapsed rubber hose; stuck wheel cylinder | Lift vehicle; spin each wheel by hand; compare drag | Lubricate slide pins; replace hose; rebuild caliper/cylinder |
| 7 | **Brake drag** | Stuck caliper piston; parking-cable over-adjusted; failed return spring | Hand-spin wheel after release; rotor temperature | Rebuild caliper; adjust cable; replace spring |

*(Any six rows from the above table earn full 7 marks.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**Operation of ABS (Block Description):**
An Anti-lock Braking System uses **four wheel-speed sensors**, an **ABS Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, and a **hydraulic modulator unit** (containing an electric pump and solenoid valves) to prevent wheel lock-up during emergency braking.

**Functional sequence:**
1. **Sensors** continuously monitor each wheel's rotational speed.
2. **ECU** compares individual wheel speeds; if a wheel decelerates too rapidly (indicating impending lock-up), it sends commands to the **modulator**.
3. **Modulator** performs one of three actions on that wheel's brake circuit:
   - **Hold** pressure (no further increase).
   - **Decrease** pressure (release brake on that wheel).
   - **Increase** pressure (re-apply brake).
4. This cycle occurs **15–25 times per second** — faster than any human reaction — preventing lock-up and maintaining steering control.

**Three common ABS faults and diagnostics:**

| # | Fault | Symptom | Diagnostic Step |
|---|-------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Wheel-speed sensor contaminated/damaged** | ABS warning lamp on; possible loss of ABS function | OBD-II scan → read sensor DTC; inspect sensor tip for iron filings; measure sensor resistance (typically 800–1 400 Ω inductive type) |
| 2 | **Sensor air-gap out of spec** | ABS warning lamp on; intermittent ABS activation | Measure gap with feeler gauge (typical 0.4–1.0 mm); adjust or replace hub/sensor |
| 3 | **ABS hydraulic modulator solenoid failure** | ABS warning lamp on; ABS inoperative; possible pressure-leak fault | Scan for solenoid DTC; measure solenoid winding resistance; perform hydraulic-actuator test via scan tool |

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing "soft pedal" with "low pedal."** A *soft* pedal feels spongy (air). A *low* pedal travels further than expected but is firm (worn pads or rear-drum self-adjuster not working). Many students write the same cause for both — examiners deduct 2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Not specifying the bleed sequence.** When asked to bleed the system, the answer must include the *order* (typically longest line first, or per OEM — usually RR → LR → RF → LF on a diagonal-split system). Writing only "bleed the brakes" loses 2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Recommending brake-fluid types incorrectly.** DOT 3, 4, 5.1 are glycol-based and *miscible*. DOT 5 is silicone and *must never be mixed* with the others. Citing "DOT 5" as a generic replacement loses 2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Forgetting the cast "MIN TH." on the disc.** A disc can never be resurfaced below its minimum thickness. Students who write "resurface the disc" without checking the MIN TH. mark lose 2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Mixing up disc and drum service limits.** Discs are *resurfaced* (within thickness limits); drums are *replaced* once they exceed MAX DIA — drums are not normally resurfaced in modern workshops. Confusing these costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this before the exam.**

- ✅ **Five cardinal brake symptoms:** Squeal / Grinding / Soft pedal / Hard pedal / Pull / Pulsation — know the top-3 causes of each.
- ✅ **Service limits to memorise:** Pad $h_{\min} = 2.0$ mm; Disc runout $\leq 0.05$ mm; Disc MIN TH. *cast on disc*; Drum MAX DIA. *cast on drum*.
- ✅ **Brake-fluid specs:** DOT 3 (205 °C dry / 140 °C wet); DOT 4 (230 / 155); DOT 5.1 (260 / 180); DOT 5 (260 / 180, silicone, *never mix*).
- ✅ **Booster gain** typically $3$–$6\times$; if hard pedal → check vacuum first.
- ✅ **Tandem master cylinder** provides diagonal-split redundancy — single-circuit failure still gives braking on opposite diagonal pair.
- ✅ **Stopping-distance formula:** $d_{\text{brake}} = v^2 / (2 \mu g)$ — **always convert km/h → m/s**.
- ✅ **Total stopping distance:** $d_{\text{total}} = v \cdot t_r + v^2 / (2 \mu g)$.
- ✅ **ABS fault diagnosis** always begins with OBD-II scan → DTC → live data → targeted test.
- ✅ **Bleed sequence (typical diagonal-split):** RR → LR → RF → LF.
- ✅ **Disc vs Drum service philosophy:** Discs can be resurfaced within MIN TH.; Drums are *replaced*, not resurfaced (in modern OEM practice).
- ✅ **Pascal's Law:** $P = F / A$ — the master cylinder converts small pedal force into high pressure; calipers convert this pressure back into large clamping force.
- ✅ **Always road-test** after any brake repair and confirm pedal reserve travel $\geq 50$ mm before returning the vehicle to the customer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tinkering, Painting, Soldering, and Metalloid Paste Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tinkering, Painting, Soldering, and Metalloid Paste Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tinkering (Panel Beating / Dent Removal)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Tinkering** is the traditional automotive body-repair process of restoring deformed sheet-metal panels (doors, fenders, bonnets, roof) to their original contour using manual hammers, dollies, mallets, and pulling equipment — *without* the application of heat or filler material. It relies on **plastic deformation** of the steel and the operator's skill in "reading" the metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tinkering is the *first* stage in any body-repair workflow. It precedes filling, soldering, and painting. Skipping it always results in a poor finish.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pressing your thumb into a soft aluminum foil ball. The dent does not vanish — but if you push the surrounding raised metal *back into the low spot* from the underside, the surface flattens again. Tinkering does exactly that on a car door: the technician feels for the high crowns around a dent and carefully hammers them back down, working the metal in reverse of how it was bent. **The metal is never destroyed; it is *persuaded***.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Tinkering is **cold work** — no flame, no filler.
> - The aim is to remove **≥ 80 %** of the dent depth *before* any filler is applied.
> - Modern equivalents use **PDR (Paintless Dent Repair)** tools, but the principle is identical.

---

## 1.2 Painting (Automotive Refinishing)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Automotive painting** is the multi-layer coating process used to protect the vehicle body from corrosion and to provide a decorative, color-matched finish. A modern refinish system consists of **primer → basecoat (color) → clearcoat**, applied over a properly prepared substrate.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Painting a car is like painting your fingernails properly: you cannot skip the *base coat* (nail hardener/primer), the *colour coat* (the polish), or the *top coat* (the gloss). Each layer has a job, and if the nail underneath is oily or rough, the polish peels in two days. The same is true for a car — **surface preparation is 90 % of a quality paint job**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** A high-quality automotive paint finish should retain gloss and color for a minimum of **5 years** under normal atmospheric exposure (per **IS 101 – Methods of Sampling and Test for Paints**).

---

## 1.3 Soldering (Body Soldering / Leading)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Soldering** in automotive body work is the process of filling dents, seams, and joints using a low-melting-point **tin–lead alloy** (historically 40/60 or 50/50 Sn/Pb) heated with a torch and a soldering iron/copper bit, often called **"leading"** because of the lead content. It produces a smooth, hard surface that can be filed and shaped before painting.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of soldering body panels like *plastering a wall crack* — but instead of wet plaster, you use molten metal "butter" that hardens and can be sanded flat. The solder sticks to clean bare metal and fills deep valleys that hammering alone cannot fix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Soldering temperature: **200 °C – 250 °C** (well below steel's melting point of ~1500 °C).
> - The car body steel is **not melted** — only the filler alloy is.
> - Modern workshops increasingly replace lead solder with **plastic fillers / metalloid paste** due to health concerns.

---

## 1.4 Metalloid Paste Application (Body Fillers / Putty)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Metalloid paste** (commonly called **body filler**, **putty**, or **polyester filler**) is a two-component paste — a polyester resin base loaded with talc, glass microspheres, or metallic powders, mixed with a hardener (organic peroxide, typically **MEKP – Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide**) — used to fill minor surface irregularities, scratches, and small dents on vehicle body panels before painting.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Metalloid paste is to a car panel what **spackle** is to a drywall — a creamy paste you trowel on, let harden, then sand smooth. The "metalloid" name comes from the metal-like dust (talc/aluminum powder) inside that makes it tougher than wall spackle and able to withstand vibration and heat on a moving vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mixing Ratio (critical):** Typically **100 : 2** by weight (resin : hardener). Too much hardener → brittle, cracked filler. Too little → never sets.

---

## 1.5 Workflow Position of Each Process

| Stage | Process | Purpose |
|:------|:--------|:--------|
| 1 | Damage assessment | Decide repair strategy |
| 2 | **Tinkering** | Remove major dents by reshaping |
| 3 | **Soldering** (if applicable) | Fill deep dents / weld seams |
| 4 | **Metalloid paste** | Fill minor irregularities |
| 5 | Sanding & masking | Prepare for paint |
| 6 | **Painting** | Primer → Basecoat → Clearcoat |
| 7 | Polishing & finishing | Final gloss & inspection |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tinkering — Theoretical Breakdown

### Why and How It Works
1. **Why a dent forms:** Impact energy exceeds the elastic limit of the steel → permanent (plastic) deformation.
2. **Why it can be reversed:** Steel has high ductility; the displaced metal is still in the panel, just *displaced*, not lost.
3. **The "high crown" technique:** Dents produce raised crowns around the low spot. Hammering the *high points* down with a **planishing hammer** (flat, slightly crowned face) supported from behind by a **dolly** redistributes the metal back into the low area.
4. **Working order:** Always work from the **outside of the dent inward**, in a **spiral** pattern, hammering lightly. Heavy blows stretch the metal.

### Tools of Tinkering
- **Mallet** (rawhide, plastic, or wooden) — for shaping without marring.
- **Planishing hammer** — for finishing flat surfaces.
- **Dolly** — anvil held on the opposite side; comes in many shapes (heel, toe, general-purpose, half-moon).
- **Body file / cheese grater file** — for checking surface trueness and removing high spots.
- **Slide hammer / dent puller** — for dents not accessible from behind.
- **Hydraulic frame straightener** — for severe structural damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** Tinkering relies on the principle of **elastic recovery**. Even after plastic deformation, a small amount of the impact strain is stored elastically. Skilled hammering allows this elastic energy to "pull" the metal back toward its original shape — this is why **warm metal** (mildly heated with a torch in winter) tinkers more easily.

---

## 2.2 Painting — Theoretical Breakdown

### The Five Stages of a Refinish Job
1. **Washing & degreasing** — remove wax, oil, road tar (use **wax & grease remover**, NOT solvent, which can damage existing paint).
2. **Sanding / scuffing** — create a mechanical key. Grades:
   - **P80–P180** for bare metal and filler.
   - **P320–P400** for primer sanding.
   - **P1000–P1500** for clearcoat nib sanding.
3. **Masking** — cover glass, trim, tires. Use **butyl tape** at edges for clean lines.
4. **Spray application** — HVLP or conventional gun, distance **150–250 mm**, overlap **50 %**, two wet coats for base, two for clear.
5. **Curing & polishing** — bake at **60 °C for 30 min** (infrared or oven) or air-dry 24 h.

### Paint Types Used in Modern Vehicles

| Paint Type | Code | Layers | Properties |
|:-----------|:-----|:-------|:-----------|
| **Solid (acrylic enamel)** | SE | Mono | Cheap, limited gloss |
| **Metallic basecoat + clearcoat** | BC/CC | 2 | Sparkle, UV resistant |
| **Pearl / Xirallic** | 3-stage BC/CC | 3 | Premium look, color-shift |
| **Waterborne basecoat** | WB | 2 | Low VOC, eco-friendly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Fact:** Since **2010**, most European OEMs (BMW, VW, Mercedes) and Indian manufacturers exporting to EU use **waterborne basecoats** to comply with **VOC < 420 g/L** regulations (EU Directive 2004/42/EC).

---

## 2.3 Soldering — Theoretical Breakdown

### Why and How It Works
1. **Surface preparation:** The metal must be **bright and clean** — solder will not adhere to paint, rust, or oil.
2. **Tinning:** The cleaned area is first coated with a thin layer of solder using **soldering flux** (typically **zinc-chloride or rosin-based**). This is called **tinning**.
3. **Application:** The heated **soldering copper** (a large iron-shaped copper bit) carries molten solder to the panel. The panel itself is warmed with a torch to keep the solder molten long enough to spread.
4. **Shaping:** While still warm, the solder is smoothed with a **wooden paddle** dipped in flux. After cooling, it is filed to contour.

### Types of Solder Used in Body Work

| Solder Type | Composition | Melting Point | Use |
|:------------|:------------|:--------------|:----|
| **Tin–lead (50/50)** | 50% Sn, 50% Pb | ~215 °C | Traditional body solder |
| **Tin–lead (40/60)** | 40% Sn, 60% Pb | ~235 °C | Older radiator work |
| **Lead-free** | Sn–Ag, Sn–Cu | ~227 °C | Modern, health-safe |
| **Silver solder (hard)** | Ag–Cu–Zn | > 600 °C | Structural, *brazing* not soldering |

> [!NOTE]
> Strictly, **silver solder work** is **brazing**, since it exceeds 450 °C. KTU examiners often ask this distinction.

### Flux Function
Flux performs three jobs:
- **Dissolves** surface oxides chemically.
- **Prevents** re-oxidation during heating by forming a protective skin.
- **Improves** wetting — allows solder to flow and bond uniformly.

---

## 2.4 Metalloid Paste — Theoretical Breakdown

### Why and How It Works
1. **Polyester resin base** is thixotropic — it flows when spread but stays put on vertical surfaces.
2. **MEKP hardener** triggers **free-radical polymerization**, converting the liquid resin to a hard, cross-linked solid in **8–15 minutes** at room temperature.
3. **Fillers** (talc CaCO₃, glass beads, aluminum powder) reduce cost, control shrinkage, and improve sanding.
4. **Heat sensitivity:** The **exothermic reaction** can raise the paste temperature to **80–100 °C** if over-catalyzed.

### Why It Cannot Replace Tinkering
Metalloid paste is for **surface irregularities up to ~3 mm deep**. For deeper dents, relying on filler alone:
- Adds **weight** (unevenly).
- **Cracks** under vibration.
- Is **expensive** (filler costs more per kg than steel).
- Loses adhesion over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Body Repair (KTU-favourite exam line):**
> *"Metal should be restored with metal; filler is only for what the hammer cannot reach."*

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Data Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit | Formula / Note |
|:-:|:----------|:--------------|:-----|:---------------|
| 1 | Tin–lead eutectic melting point | $T_m$ | °C | $T_m = 183\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (63/37 eutectic) |
| 2 | Solder working temperature | $T_w$ | °C | $T_w = T_m + (40\ \text{to}\ 60) \approx 220\text{–}240\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 3 | Steel panel melting point | $T_{steel}$ | °C | $T_{steel} \approx 1500\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 4 | Filler hardener ratio | $R_h$ | % by weight | $R_h = \dfrac{m_{MEKP}}{m_{resin}} \times 100 \approx 1.5\%\text{–}3\%$ |
| 5 | Filler pot life | $t_{pot}$ | min | $t_{pot} = 8\text{–}15$ at $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 6 | Spray gun distance | $d$ | mm | $d = 150\text{–}250$ |
| 7 | Spray overlap | $\eta_{ov}$ | % | $\eta_{ov} = 50$ |
| 8 | VOC limit (EU) | $\rho_{VOC}$ | g/L | $\rho_{VOC} < 420$ |
| 9 | Spray air pressure (HVLP) | $P_{air}$ | bar | $P_{air} = 2.0\text{–}2.5$ |
| 10 | Film thickness (basecoat) | $\delta_{bc}$ | µm | $\delta_{bc} = 15\text{–}25$ |
| 11 | Film thickness (clearcoat) | $\delta_{cc}$ | µm | $\delta_{cc} = 40\text{–}60$ |
| 12 | Sandpaper grit progression | $G$ | – | $G: 80 \rightarrow 180 \rightarrow 320 \rightarrow 600 \rightarrow 1000 \rightarrow 1500$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever the question mentions a temperature for soldering/tinning, write **~ 230 °C** for 40/60 or **~ 215 °C** for 50/50 solder. Do **not** confuse soldering with brazing/welding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Heat-Transfer Derivation for Solder Working Temperature

**Problem:** Show mathematically why the soldering copper must be heated *above* the solder's melting point.

The heat balance on a molten solder drop on a steel panel (1-D transient conduction, lumped capacitance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heat required to melt solder} &: \quad Q_m = m_s \cdot L_f \\
\text{Heat supplied by copper bit} &: \quad Q_c = m_c \cdot c_c \cdot \Delta T \\
\text{Heat lost to steel panel} &: \quad Q_p = m_p \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_p
\end{aligned}
$$

At equilibrium (steady state, no loss):

$$
Q_c = Q_m + Q_p
$$

$$
m_c \, c_c \, (T_{bit} - T_m) = m_s \, L_f + m_p \, c_p \, (T_m - T_{panel})
$$

**Numerical example:** A 2 kg copper bit ( $c_c = 0.385\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ ), soldering **50 g of 50/50 solder** ( $L_f = 30\ \text{kJ/kg}$ ) onto a 5 kg steel panel ( $c_p = 0.49\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ ), starting panel at 25 °C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS: } & 2 \times 0.385 \times (T_{bit} - 215) \;\; \text{kJ} \\
\text{RHS: } & (0.050 \times 30) + (5 \times 0.49 \times (215 - 25)) \\
& = 1.5 + 465.5 = 467 \;\;\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
0.77 \,(T_{bit} - 215) = 467
$$

$$
T_{bit} - 215 = \dfrac{467}{0.77} \approx 606.5
$$

$$
T_{bit} \approx 215 + 606.5 = 821.5 \;^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Conclusion:** The copper soldering bit must be heated to **~ 820 °C** to supply enough enthalpy to melt solder *and* warm the steel panel to bonding temperature. In practice, **direct torch heating of the panel** is added so the bit only needs **300–400 °C** — this is the standard workshop method.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a KTU favourite numerical — examiners often give masses and ask for the copper bit temperature.

---

## 3.2 Calculation of Filler Quantity Needed

**Problem:** A car fender has a dent of effective area **0.05 m²** and average depth **2 mm**. Density of cured filler ≈ **1.6 g/cm³**. Find the mass of filler required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A \times d \\
V &= 0.05 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \\
V &= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\text{m}^3 = 100 \;\;\text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho \times V \\
m &= 1.6 \times 100 \\
m &= 160 \;\;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Including ~ 20 % waste (sanding, mixing on board, overflow):

$$
m_{actual} = 160 \times 1.2 = 192 \;\;\text{g}
$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **200 g** of filler is needed. Workshop packs are sold in **250 g, 500 g, 1 kg, 4 kg** containers — so a **250 g tin** suffices.

---

## 3.3 Spray Gun Parameter Analysis

**Problem:** An HVLP spray gun with **1.4 mm nozzle** operates at **2.5 bar** inlet pressure. Calculate the theoretical spray fan width at a distance of **200 mm** and the overlap needed.

The fan width at distance $d$ for a given flow rate $Q$ and spray angle $\theta$:

$$
W = 2 \, d \, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

For a typical HVLP gun, $\theta = 30^\circ$:

$$
W = 2 \times 200 \times \tan(15^\circ)
$$

$$
W = 400 \times 0.268 = 107.2 \;\;\text{mm} \approx 110 \;\;\text{mm}
$$

For **50 % overlap**, each pass advances by:

$$
\Delta x = W \times 0.5 = 110 \times 0.5 = 55 \;\;\text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Painter should move the gun in **55 mm steps** for uniform coverage. KTU students should remember the **50 % overlap rule**.

---

## 3.4 Tinkering — Strain-Energy Perspective

When a panel is dented, plastic strain energy is stored:

$$
U_p = \int \sigma_y \, \varepsilon_p \, dV
$$

Where $\sigma_y$ = yield strength of mild steel ≈ **250 MPa**, $\varepsilon_p$ = plastic strain.

When the technician hammers the high crowns back, **negative plastic work** is done on the panel, releasing strain energy. The residual dent after imperfect tinkering has:

$$
U_{residual} = U_p - W_{hammer}
$$

Where $W_{hammer}$ = work done by hammering. This is why a skilled tinsmith can restore **80–95 %** of the original shape.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection

| Damage Severity | Best Process | Why | Cost Index |
|:----------------|:-------------|:----|:-----------|
| Scratch < 0.1 mm | Compound & polish only | No paint removal | 1× |
| Minor scuff, paint intact | Wet sand + touch-up paint | Preserves OEM paint | 2× |
| Shallow dent 1–3 mm, no paint crack | Tinkering + metalloid paste | Metal work + minimal filler | 3× |
| Medium dent 3–10 mm, paint cracked | Tinkering + solder + filler | Multi-stage restoration | 5× |
| Deep dent > 10 mm, panel stretched | Section replacement + welding | Beyond repair economics | 10× |
| Rust perforation | Cut out, weld patch, full repaint | Structural integrity | 12× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Index** is approximate and assumes a hatchback panel as reference (1×). KTU examiners appreciate when students link process selection to *economics*, not just technique.

---

## 3.6 Paint Film Build-Up Calculation

For a 3-coat system (primer + base + clear):

$$
\delta_{total} = \delta_{primer} + \delta_{base} + \delta_{clear}
$$

$$
\delta_{total} = (40\text{–}60) + (15\text{–}25) + (40\text{–}60)
$$

$$
\delta_{total} \approx 95\text{–}145 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

Typical **OEM paint thickness on a new car: 100–150 µm**. A refinish should match this within ± 20 µm to avoid colour mismatch and stone-chip weakness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Body-Repair Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Entry & Damage Assessment] --> B[Disassembly of Trim & Glass]
    B --> C[Tinkering Hammering & Planishing]
    C --> D{Dent depth > 3 mm?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Soldering / Leading]
    D -- No --> F[Surface Sanding P80-P180]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Metalloid Paste Application]
    G --> H[Hardening Wait 30 min]
    H --> I[Sand P320-P400]
    I --> J[Masking & Degreasing]
    J --> K[Primer Spray 2 coats]
    K --> L[Sand P600]
    L --> M[Basecoat Spray 2 coats]
    M --> N[Clearcoat Spray 2 coats]
    N --> O[Bake 60C 30 min]
    O --> P[Polishing & Inspection]
    P --> Q[Final Delivery]
```

## 4.2 Tinkering Tool & Force Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tinkering Operation
        A[Hammer Face] -->|Impact Force F| B[Sheet Metal Panel]
        B --> C[High Crown Raised Area]
        C --> D[Dolly Support from Underside]
        D -->|Reaction Force R| B
    end
    B -.->|Plastic flow back| E[Original Panel Surface]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dolly is not just a passive anvil — it determines the *contour* of the finished surface. Different dolly shapes (heel, toe, double-bevel) give different finishes.

## 4.3 Soldering Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clean Panel with Emery Cloth] --> B[Apply Zinc Chloride Flux]
    B --> C[Heat Panel with Torch 200C]
    C --> D[Apply Heated Copper Bit with Solder]
    D --> E[Tinning Layer Forms]
    E --> F[Build Up Solder Layer to Fill Dent]
    F --> G[Smooth with Wooden Paddle + Flux]
    G --> H[Cool Slowly]
    H --> I[File to Final Contour]
    I --> J[Sand P80-P180 for Next Stage]
```

## 4.4 Metalloid Paste — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Resin Base
        R1[Polyester Resin]
        R2[Talc CaCO3 Filler]
        R3[Glass Microspheres]
        R4[Thixotropic Agent]
    end
    subgraph Hardener Tube
        H1[MEKP Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide]
        H2[Plasticizer]
    end
    R1 --> MIX[Mixing Board 100:2 by Weight]
    R2 --> MIX
    R3 --> MIX
    R4 --> MIX
    H1 --> MIX
    H2 --> MIX
    MIX -->|Pot life 8-15 min| APPLY[Apply with Spreader]
    APPLY --> CURE[Exothermic Cure]
    CURE --> SAND[Sand P80-P180]
```

## 4.5 Painting — Three-Layer Refinish System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Substrate
        S[Bare Metal / Filler / Old Paint]
    end
    S --> P1[Primer Surfacer 40-60 um]
    P1 --> P2[Basecoat Color 15-25 um]
    P2 --> P3[Clearcoat 40-60 um]
    P3 --> P4[Cured Film 95-145 um Total]
    
    P1 -.->|Function| F1[Corrosion Protection + Filling]
    P2 -.->|Function| F2[Color + Metallic Effect]
    P3 -.->|Function| F3[Gloss + UV Protection]
```

## 4.6 Decision Tree — Soldering vs. Metalloid Paste

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Dent Identified] --> Q1{Panel Area Hot Work Allowed?}
    Q1 -- No --> MP[Use Metalloid Paste]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Dent Depth?}
    Q2 -- Less than 3 mm --> MP
    Q2 -- 3 to 8 mm --> Q3{Workshop has Lead Solder Skill?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SOLD[Soldering Recommended]
    Q3 -- No --> MP
    Q2 -- More than 8 mm --> REPL[Section Replacement]
    
    MP --> NOTE[Modern Workshops Prefer This]
    SOLD --> NOTE2[Traditional Method Skilled Labour]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students should be able to draw a **flowchart** of body repair workflow in the exam. The above six diagrams are the canonical ones to memorize.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define tinkering. List any four tools used in the process.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Tinkering is the process of reshaping damaged sheet-metal body panels back to their original contour using hand hammers, mallets, and dollies *without* the application of heat or filler material. It relies on plastic deformation and elastic recovery of the steel.

**Tools (1.5 marks — any four):**
1. Rawhide / wooden / plastic mallet.
2. Planishing hammer.
3. Dolly (general-purpose, heel, or toe type).
4. Body file (cheese-grater file).
5. Slide hammer / dent puller.
6. Spoon or pry bar.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is metalloid paste? State the function of the hardener used in it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Metalloid paste, also called body filler or putty, is a two-component paste consisting of a polyester resin base loaded with inert fillers (talc, glass microspheres, or metallic powders) and a separate hardener. It is used to fill minor dents, surface scratches, and irregularities on vehicle panels before painting.

**Hardener function (1.5 marks):** The hardener is **Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide (MEKP)**, an organic peroxide that initiates free-radical polymerization of the polyester resin at room temperature, converting the soft paste into a hard, sandable solid in 8–15 minutes.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]** With neat sketches, explain in detail:
**(a)** The step-by-step procedure of **tinkering** a car door panel that has a shallow dent. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The process of **soldering** body panels — including types of solder, fluxes used, and applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Tinkering Procedure for a Shallow Door Dent (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Damage assessment (1 mark):** Inspect the dent from outside (oblique light shows high and low spots) and from inside (access for dolly placement). Identify the high crown and the low spot.

**Step 2 — Access preparation (0.5 mark):** Remove interior trim and door panel to allow dolly access from behind.

**Step 3 — Initial roughing-out (1 mark):** Use a **slide hammer or knock-down tool** to pull the deepest part of the dent outward until it is roughly flush with the surrounding metal.

**Step 4 — Planishing (1.5 marks):** With the dolly held behind the panel opposite the dent, use a **planishing hammer** to tap lightly on the high crowns. Work in a spiral pattern from the outside inward. The dolly provides backing so the panel does not stretch.

**Step 5 — Body filing (1 mark):** Run a **body file** diagonally across the panel — it removes metal only from the high spots, leaving the low spots untouched. This confirms trueness.

**Step 6 — Final finishing (1 mark):** Use a soft mallet for final contour. The surface should now be within **0.5 mm** of original shape; remaining minor imperfections will be handled by metalloid paste.

**Step 7 — Cleaning (1 mark):** Wipe with wax-and-grease remover before next stage.

---

#### (b) Soldering Process for Body Panels (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Soldering (or "leading") fills dents that are too deep for filler alone. It uses a **tin–lead alloy** melted at **200–250 °C** and applied to clean, flux-coated steel.

**Types of solder (2 marks):**
| Type | Composition | Melting Point | Use |
|:-----|:------------|:--------------|:----|
| 50/50 tin–lead | 50% Sn, 50% Pb | ~215 °C | Body filling |
| 40/60 tin–lead | 40% Sn, 60% Pb | ~235 °C | Older radiator work |
| Lead-free | Sn–Cu / Sn–Ag | ~227 °C | Health-safe modern |

**Fluxes used (1 mark):** **Zinc chloride** (most active, for steel), **rosin** (mild, for electrical), **organic acid fluxes** (intermediate).

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Clean** the dent area with emery cloth down to bright metal.
2. **Apply flux** with a brush.
3. **Heat the panel** gently with a propane or oxy-acetylene torch until flux bubbles.
4. **Tin** the area: rub with the heated soldering copper loaded with solder — a thin silvery layer should wet the steel.
5. **Build up solder** by dragging the copper across the dent, adding solder until the low spot is filled slightly proud.
6. **Smooth** with a wooden paddle dipped in flux while still plastic.
7. **File** to contour when cool.
8. **Sand** with P80 → P180 before filler application.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing the solder melting point as **> 400 °C** — this is brazing, not soldering. Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting to mention **flux** — examiners deduct 1 mark because flux is essential to bond formation.
> 3. Confusing **tinning** (pre-coating step) with **soldering** (filling step).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Explain in detail:
**(a)** The complete **automotive painting procedure** including surface preparation, types of paint, and spray technique. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** **Metalloid paste** — its composition, application procedure, and the precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Automotive Painting Procedure (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Surface preparation (2 marks):**
- Wash with soap and water.
- Degrease with wax & grease remover (NOT solvent).
- Sand with progressive grits: P80 (bare metal) → P180 → P320 (filler) → P400 (primer).
- Blow off dust with air blower; tack-cloth wipe.

**Step 2 — Masking (0.5 mark):** Cover glass, trim, lights, tyres using masking paper, plastic sheeting, and butyl tape at edges.

**Step 3 — Primer application (1.5 marks):**
- Mix primer with hardener and thinner per manufacturer's TDS (e.g., 4:1:1 by volume).
- Spray **2 wet coats** at 2.0–2.5 bar HVLP, 150–250 mm distance, 50 % overlap.
- Allow 10 min flash-off between coats.
- Bake 60 °C / 30 min or air-dry 4 hours.
- Sand with **P600** wet before basecoat.

**Step 4 — Basecoat (1 mark):** Apply 2 coats of colour (metallic or solid), 15–25 µm dry film. Allow 10–15 min flash-off.

**Step 5 — Clearcoat (1 mark):** Apply 2 wet coats, 40–60 µm dry film. Bake 60 °C / 30 min for full cure.

**Step 6 — Polishing (1 mark):** After 24 h, compound with P1500 → P3000 → polish → glaze for OEM-level gloss.

**Paint types covered:** Solid, metallic basecoat + clearcoat (BC/CC), pearl, waterborne (low-VOC).

---

#### (b) Metalloid Paste — Composition, Application, Precautions (7 Marks)

**Composition (2 marks):**
| Component | Function | Typical % |
|:----------|:---------|:---------|
| Polyester resin (unsaturated) | Binder, polymerizes to hard solid | 30–40 % |
| Talc / CaCO₃ / glass beads | Bulk filler, reduces shrinkage | 50–60 % |
| Thixotropic agent (e.g., fumed silica) | Prevents sagging on verticals | 1–2 % |
| Pigments (grey, beige) | Visual identification | < 1 % |
| **Hardener (MEKP)** | Initiates cure (in separate tube) | 1.5–3 % by weight |

**Application procedure (3 marks):**
1. Sand dent area to P80; clean and degrease.
2. On a clean mixing board, dispense the required filler.
3. Squeeze **2 % hardener** by weight from the tube; mix thoroughly with a spreader for **45 seconds** until uniform colour (no streaks).
4. Apply with a flexible spreader, pressing firmly into the dent in **thin layers** (max 3 mm each).
5. Allow to harden — usually **15–20 minutes** at 25 °C. Surface becomes warm due to exotherm.
6. Rough-shape with a body file, then sand P80 → P180 → P320 before primer.

**Precautions (2 marks):**
- **Do not** mix more than can be used in 5 minutes (pot life is short).
- **Do not** exceed 3 % hardener — causes brittleness and cracking.
- Avoid application in direct sunlight (shortens pot life drastically).
- Do not apply over **un-sanded old paint, primer, or plastic** — adhesion fails.
- Always wear **nitrile gloves** — MEKP is a skin irritant.
- Store tins tightly closed; hardener is flammable and oxidizing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing "**metallic** powder" as the main filler of metalloid paste — the resin + talc are main components; metallic powder is *optional* and a distractor.
> 2. Saying the filler "dries" — it actually **cures by chemical reaction**, not by drying. Wrong terminology loses 1 mark.
> 3. Omitting the **temperature warning** — many students forget that exothermic heat can warp thin panels.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Tinkering**
  - Cold work; no heat, no filler.
  - Tool sequence: **assess → access → pull → planish → file → finish**.
  - Principle: high crowns hammered down, supported by dolly.
  - Goal: remove ≥ 80 % of dent *with metal*.

- **Painting**
  - **Three layers**: Primer (40–60 µm) + Basecoat (15–25 µm) + Clearcoat (40–60 µm).
  - Total film: **95–145 µm**.
  - Spray distance **150–250 mm**, overlap **50 %**, HVLP pressure **2.0–2.5 bar**.
  - VOC limit (EU): **< 420 g/L** → use waterborne basecoats.
  - Bake **60 °C / 30 min** for full cure.

- **Soldering**
  - Solder melts at **200–250 °C**, well below steel (1500 °C).
  - Common alloys: **50/50 Sn-Pb (~215 °C)** or **40/60 (~235 °C)**.
  - **Flux** = zinc chloride for steel.
  - Steps: clean → flux → heat panel → tin → fill → smooth → file → sand.
  - > 450 °C = **brazing**, not soldering.

- **Metalloid Paste**
  - Two-component: **polyester resin + MEKP hardener**.
  - Mix ratio **100 : 2** by weight.
  - Pot life **8–15 min**; cure by **exothermic polymerization** (not drying).
  - Max layer thickness **~3 mm**; deeper dents need multiple layers.
  - Storage: cool, dry, tightly closed (MEKP is oxidizing).
  - PPE: nitrile gloves + safety glasses.

- **Golden Rule:** *"Restore metal with metal; filler only for what the hammer cannot reach."*

- **Decision rule:**
  - Dent < 0.1 mm → polish only.
  - 0.1–3 mm → tinkering + filler.
  - 3–8 mm → tinkering + solder + filler.
  - > 8 mm → section replacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module 3 carries roughly **15–20 %** weightage in the End-Semester Exam. Of this, **painting and solder types** are asked almost every semester, while **metalloid paste precautions** is a high-probability 7-mark question. Practise sketching the workflow flowchart and the spray-gun overlap diagram — both are *very* frequently asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyre and Wheel Maintenance: Rotation, Balancing, and Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tyre and Wheel Maintenance: Rotation, Balancing, and Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tyre and Wheel Maintenance — Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT635)**, *Tyre and Wheel Maintenance* refers to the systematic set of preventive and corrective service operations performed on the rolling assembly of an automobile (tyre + wheel + hub) to ensure **uniform tread wear, predictable directional stability, vibration-free ride, optimal fuel efficiency, and extended component life**. The three pillars of this discipline are:

1. **Tyre Rotation** — Periodic repositioning of tyres among wheel positions to equalise wear rate.
2. **Wheel Balancing** — Correction of mass distribution irregularities in the tyre-wheel assembly to eliminate vertical (static) and couple (dynamic) vibrations.
3. **Wheel Alignment** — Geometric adjustment of suspension angles (Camber, Caster, Toe, SAI) to manufacturer-specified tolerances so that wheels roll true and steer accurately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** These three operations are *distinct* service procedures but are *interdependent* in their effect on tyre wear. A perfectly balanced wheel that is misaligned will still wear out prematurely, and a perfectly aligned wheel with imbalance will still vibrate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are walking in **new leather shoes**.

- **Tyre Rotation** is like **swapping your shoes between left and right feet every day** — both shoes wear down at the same rate, so neither becomes lopsided before the other.
- **Wheel Balancing** is like **putting a tiny weight on a spinning ceiling fan that wobbles** — once the rotational mass is symmetrical, the wobble disappears and the fan runs silently.
- **Wheel Alignment** is like **getting the wheels of a shopping trolley adjusted so it rolls straight** when you push it, instead of veering to one side and rubbing against the kerb.

In an automobile, the **tyre is the only contact patch** (roughly the size of a postcard) connecting a 1500 kg vehicle to the road. Any irregularity in this contact patch — be it uneven wear, vibration, or wrong angle — translates directly into safety risks, comfort loss, and higher running cost.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Tolerances

The following metrics are universally accepted as **industry standard values** used by KTU examiners and service workshops:

| Parameter | Typical Passenger Car Value | Heavy Commercial Vehicle Value |
|---|---|---|
| Camber angle | **−0.5° to +1.0°** | **0° to +1.5°** |
| Caster angle | **+2.0° to +5.0°** | **+3.0° to +6.0°** |
| Toe-in (front) | **0° to +0.2°** (0–6 mm) | **0° to +0.3°** |
| Steering Axis Inclination (SAI) | **10° to 15°** | **5° to 8°** |
| Maximum static imbalance | **≤ 5 grams** | **≤ 25 grams** |
| Acceptable radial run-out | **≤ 1.0 mm** | **≤ 2.0 mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> All wheel alignment angles are referenced from the **true vertical (gravity line)** and measured with the vehicle on a level surface, kerb weight, with fuel tank full and tyres at recommended cold inflation pressure.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, this topic integrates concepts from **Strength of Materials** (statically indeterminate reactions), **Engineering Mechanics** (force couples, rotational dynamics), and **Automotive Engineering** (suspension geometry). Examiners frequently test:
- Definitions and purposes of rotation patterns.
- The physics of static vs. dynamic imbalance.
- The geometric meaning of camber, caster, and toe.
- The relationship between misalignment symptoms and the angle responsible.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tyre Rotation — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Why Tyres Wear Unevenly

Tyres do not wear uniformly because of **non-uniform load distribution, steering duties, and drivetrain torque**. On a typical front-engine, front-wheel-drive (FF) car:

- **Front tyres** carry ~60 % of vehicle weight and perform both *steering* and *driving* → wear fastest on the **outer shoulder** (due to cornering slip).
- **Rear tyres** carry less load, only follow → wear evenly across the tread.

**Result:** Front tyres may need replacement at 30,000 km while rears last 50,000 km. **Rotation equalises this.**

### 2.1.2 Standard Rotation Patterns

Five approved rotation patterns are recognised worldwide. The choice depends on whether the vehicle is **front-wheel-drive (FWD), rear-wheel-drive (RWD), all-wheel-drive (AWD), or has directional/non-directional tyres**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Directional tyres** (with asymmetric tread pattern) can **only** be swapped front-to-rear on the same side. They cannot be crossed.

**The five standard patterns are summarised below:**

| Pattern Name | Vehicles Using It | Movement Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Cross | FWD | FL → FR → RR → RL → FL |
| Rearward Cross | RWD | RL → RR → FR → FL → RL |
| X-Pattern (4-way Cross) | AWD / 4WD symmetrical | All four diagonal swaps |
| Front-to-Rear | Directional tyres | Same-side swap only |
| Side-to-Side | Spare tyre included (5-tire rotation) | Spare cycles into use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect the student to identify **which pattern suits which drivetrain**. RWD uses *Rearward Cross* because the driven rear tyres wear fastest on the inner edges due to thrust loads, so they must move to the front where scrub is absent.

## 2.2 Wheel Balancing — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Two Types of Imbalance

A tyre-wheel assembly has **two distinct modes of imbalance**, both arising from microscopic mass asymmetries introduced during manufacturing, moulding, or wear.

**A. Static Imbalance (Force Imbalance)**

The centre of mass of the assembly is offset from the **geometric centre of rotation** by a small radial distance *r*. When rotated at angular velocity *ω*, it produces a centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2}$$

This force is **vertical** at the top of the wheel, causing the classic up-down wobble at a frequency equal to the wheel rotation rate (1× wheel speed).

**B. Dynamic Imbalance (Couple Imbalance)**

Two equal masses are placed on **opposite sides** of the wheel such that the centre of mass *is* on the axis, but the **principal axis of inertia** is tilted. The result is a **wobbling couple** that produces side-to-side shake at twice the wheel rotation rate (2× wheel speed — actually 1×, but appears as steering wheel oscillation at road speed).

### 2.2.2 Mass Eccentricity Relationship

If the residual imbalance after correction is *m* grams at radius *r* mm, the unbalance grade is expressed in **gram-millimetres (g·mm)**:

$$U = m \cdot r \quad \text{[g·mm]}$$

For passenger car wheels, KTU/SAE standard **G40 grade** allows maximum **40 g·mm** per wheel.

### 2.2.3 Balancing Procedure

1. Mount wheel on a **spin balancer** (off-vehicle) or **road-force balancer** (on-vehicle).
2. Calibrate the machine using a known test weight.
3. Spin the wheel to ~120 rpm; sensors measure vibration amplitudes.
4. The machine displays **inner (inboard) and outer (outboard) correction values** in grams.
5. A technician glues or clips **adhesive weights** at the indicated angles.

## 2.3 Wheel Alignment — Theoretical Foundations

Wheel alignment is divided into **three primary angles** (set by manufacturer) and **two secondary angles** (derived from primary ones).

### 2.3.1 Primary Angles

**(a) Camber** — The tilt of the wheel from vertical when viewed from the **front** of the vehicle.
- **Positive camber** = top of wheel tilts outward.
- **Negative camber** = top of wheel tilts inward.
- Effect: counteracts the tendency of the outer edge to wear due to road crown.

$$\text{Camber angle } (\gamma) = \frac{\Delta y}{R_{wheel}}$$

where $\Delta y$ is the lateral offset of the wheel centre and $R_{wheel}$ is the wheel radius.

**(b) Caster** — The forward or rearward tilt of the steering axis from vertical, viewed from the **side** of the vehicle.
- **Positive caster** = steering axis tilts toward rear of vehicle (like a bicycle fork).
- Provides **directional stability** by creating a self-centring moment.

$$\text{Caster trail } (t) = R_{kingpin\,pin} \cdot \sin(\gamma_{caster})$$

**(c) Toe** — The difference in distance between the **front** and **rear** of the two front tyres, viewed from **above**.
- **Toe-in** = front of tyres closer together.
- **Toe-out** = front of tyres farther apart.
- Measured in **millimetres** or **degrees-minutes**.

$$\text{Toe angle } (\tau) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d_{rear} - d_{front}}{2 \cdot WB}\right)$$

where $WB$ is the wheelbase.

### 2.3.2 Secondary (Derived) Angles

**(d) Steering Axis Inclination (SAI)** — Inward tilt of the steering axis from vertical at the front.
- Generates a **lift** in the front when steering, providing natural self-centring.

**(e) Included Angle** = SAI + Camber (on the same wheel).

**(f) Scrub Radius** — Distance at the road surface between the steering axis intersection point and the tyre contact patch centre.
- Positive scrub radius: steering axis meets road *inside* the contact patch.
- Zero scrub radius: axis meets road at the centre — desirable for disc brake vehicles (no kickback).

### 2.3.3 Effect of Each Angle on Tyre Wear

| Misalignment | Direction of Wear | Visual Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| Excessive positive camber | Outer shoulder | Tyre leans outward |
| Excessive negative camber | Inner shoulder | Tyre leans inward |
| Toe-in | Both outer edges feathered | Tyres point inward |
| Toe-out | Both inner edges feathered | Tyres point outward |
| Caster mismatch (side-to-side) | Cross-camber wear | Vehicle pulls one side |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Toe misalignment causes 80 % of all premature tyre wear** in passenger vehicles. A mere 3 mm of toe misalignment can reduce tyre life by 30 %.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2}$ | Centrifugal force of imbalance | N |
| 2 | $U = m \cdot r$ | Mass unbalance product | g·mm |
| 3 | $\gamma_{camber} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{R_w}$ | Camber from offset | rad |
| 4 | $t_{trail} = r_p \cdot \sin(\gamma_{caster})$ | Mechanical trail | mm |
| 5 | $\tau_{toe} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d_r - d_f}{2\,WB}\right)$ | Toe angle | rad or deg |
| 6 | $v_{slip} = \omega \cdot R - v_{vehicle}$ | Longitudinal slip velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $\alpha_{slip} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v_y}{v_x}\right)$ | Tyre slip angle | rad |
| 8 | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | Cornering force (linear range) | N |
| 9 | $L_{life} \propto \dfrac{1}{\delta_{toe}}$ | Tyre life ∝ 1/toe error | – |
| 10 | $\theta_{vib} = \omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{v}{R_w}$ | Vibration frequency | rad/s |

Where:
- $C_\alpha$ = cornering stiffness coefficient (N/rad),
- $v_y$ = lateral velocity, $v_x$ = forward velocity,
- $\delta_{toe}$ = toe deviation from spec.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** use these alignment angles as part of the *vehicle dynamics package*; even a 0.1° change in caster can shift the steering *feel* from "sporty" to "luxury".
- **F1 race teams** optimise camber and toe lap-by-lap to extract maximum grip within thermal degradation windows.
- **Fleet operators** (KSRTC, Ola, Uber) schedule mandatory rotation + balancing every 10,000 km to lower cost-per-kilometre.
- **Tyre manufacturers** (MRF, CEAT, Michelin) recommend rotation intervals of 5,000–8,000 km to honour tread-life warranties.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Centrifugal Force from a Static Imbalance

**Given:** A passenger car wheel of mass *m_w* = 35 kg (tyre + rim + hub) has a residual imbalance of *m* = 8 grams located at radius *r* = 250 mm from the rotation axis. The car travels at *v* = 90 km/h.

**Find:** The peak vertical force exerted on the suspension at the contact patch.

### Step 1 — Convert mass to kilograms

$$m = 8 \text{ g} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Convert radius to metres

$$r = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the wheel angular velocity

Convert vehicle speed to m/s:

$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = \frac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

The wheel radius (assuming tyre size 195/65 R15 → radius ≈ 0.317 m):

$$R_{wheel} = 0.317 \text{ m}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R_{wheel}} = \frac{25}{0.317} = 78.86 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the centrifugal force equation

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2} \\
&= (8 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.250) \cdot (78.86)^{2} \\
&= (2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (6220.0) \\
&= 12.44 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the equivalent static load equivalent

For a suspension stiffness $k_s = 35,\!000 \text{ N/m}$ at the wheel:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{deflection} &= \frac{F_c}{k_s} \\
&= \frac{12.44}{35,\!000} \\
&= 3.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.355 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

A peak **vertical oscillation of ~0.36 mm** at a frequency of:

$$f_{vib} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{78.86}{2\pi} = 12.55 \text{ Hz}$$

This **matches the natural resonance of the human spine (~4–8 Hz when seated in a car with seat suspension peaking at 5 Hz)** and is felt as a steering-wheel or seat vibration. Hence KTU/SAE grade limits keep imbalance below this threshold.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mechanical Trail and Self-Centring Moment

**Given:** Caster angle $\gamma_{caster} = 3.5°$, kingpin offset $r_p = 50$ mm (distance from contact patch to steering axis along the kingpin axis projected onto the ground).

**Find:** Mechanical trail and the resulting self-centring torque at a cornering lateral force of 1500 N.

### Step 1 — Convert caster angle to radians

$$\gamma_{caster} = 3.5° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.0611 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 2 — Compute mechanical trail

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= r_p \cdot \sin(\gamma_{caster}) \\
&= 50 \cdot \sin(0.0611) \\
&= 50 \cdot 0.0610 \\
&= 3.05 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute self-centring torque

The mechanical trail acts as a lever arm. When a lateral cornering force $F_y$ acts at the contact patch, a restoring moment about the kingpin is generated:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{centre} &= F_y \cdot t \\
&= 1500 \cdot 3.05 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 4.575 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Driver perception

This torque is what causes the steering wheel to **"return to centre"** after a turn. If $t \to 0$, the steering becomes vague and unstable (zero-caster situation). If $t$ is excessive, the steering becomes heavy and the wheel tram-lines on bumps.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Toe Wear Rate using Slip Velocity

**Given:** A front wheel of radius $R_w = 0.317$ m rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ that produces a wheel linear speed $v_w = 25$ m/s, but the vehicle body moves at $v_{veh} = 24.7$ m/s due to a toe-in angle of $0.2°$.

**Find:** Slip velocity at the tyre tread surface, and quantify wear severity relative to the aligned case.

### Step 1 — Compute toe-angle lateral component of slip

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{slip} &\approx \tau_{toe} = 0.2° = 0.00349 \text{ rad} \\
v_{slip,\,lateral} &= v_{veh} \cdot \tau_{toe} \\
&= 24.7 \cdot 0.00349 \\
&= 0.0862 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the scrubbing distance per kilometre

For each kilometre of travel, the slip ratio integrated over distance gives total scrub:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{scrub} &= v_{slip,\,lateral} \cdot t_{travel} \\
&= 0.0862 \cdot \left(\frac{1000}{24.7}\right) \\
&= 0.0862 \cdot 40.49 \\
&= 3.49 \text{ m of sideways scrubbing per km}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Wear rate scaling

Empirical tyre wear studies show:

$$L_{life} \approx \frac{L_{0}}{1 + k \cdot \vert \tau_{toe} \vert}$$

where $k \approx 150$ for radial tyres and $L_0$ is nominal life.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{life} &= \frac{50,\!000}{1 + 150 \cdot 0.00349} \\
&= \frac{50,\!000}{1 + 0.524} \\
&= \frac{50,\!000}{1.524} \\
&\approx 32,\!800 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

A mere **0.2° toe-in** (which is within manufacturer spec) still **reduces tyre life by ~34 %**. This single calculation is why KTU examiners consider toe adjustment as the highest-priority alignment parameter.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linking Symptom to Root Cause

| Observed Symptom | Probable Misalignment | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Steering wheel off-centre while driving straight | Caster or camber mismatch side-to-side | Adjust cross-camber and cross-caster |
| Vehicle pulls to one side | Cross-camber or steering offset | Verify individual camber L vs R |
| Outer-edge tyre wear only on one side | Camber (sign-dependent) | Adjust camber |
| Both edges feathered equally | Toe (in or out) | Adjust toe |
| Steering wheel shimmy at 60–100 km/h | Static + dynamic imbalance | Re-balance wheel |
| Scalloped/diagonal wear pattern | Worn shock absorbers | Replace dampers + balance + rotate |
| Premature wear on driven axle only | Drivetrain torque steer | Check for incorrect rotation pattern |
| Brake pedal pulsation at high speed | Lateral run-out of disc, not wheel balance | Skim or replace brake disc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner tip:** Whenever a question gives a *symptom*, the model answer must follow the format **"Symptom → Likely cause → Diagnostic procedure → Corrective action"**. Half-marks are awarded for each correct link.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | 3 Marks]**
*Define wheel balancing. Differentiate clearly between static and dynamic imbalance.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Wheel balancing** is the process of compensating for the uneven distribution of mass in a tyre-wheel assembly so that the assembly rotates about its true geometric axis without producing centrifugal forces or couples.
>
> **Static imbalance** occurs when the centre of mass of the rotating assembly is displaced **along** the axis of rotation (radial offset). It produces a single vertical force at the heavy spot, causing an up-down vibration at wheel rotation frequency. It is corrected by adding **one weight** at the heavy spot.
>
> **Dynamic imbalance** occurs when two equal masses lie on **opposite sides** of the wheel, making the principal axis of inertia tilt relative to the rotation axis. It produces a **couple** (two equal, opposite, parallel forces) causing side-to-side wobble. It requires **two weights** on opposite sides for correction.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**
*List the standard rotation patterns for tyres and state the interval (in km) recommended for rotation.*

**Model Answer:**

> The five standard patterns are: **Forward Cross, Rearward Cross, X-Pattern (4-way Cross), Front-to-Rear (directional tyres), and 5-Tire Side-to-Side Rotation**.
>
> The recommended rotation interval is **every 5,000 to 10,000 km** or at every **scheduled oil change service**, whichever comes first. Manufacturer warranty conditions for tyres typically mandate rotation every 8,000 km. Off-road and commercial vehicles may require rotation every 5,000 km due to harsher duty cycles.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each**. **Either (a) or (b)** within a sub-choice may be selected. Below, **Question A and Question B are entirely independent choices**, each fully answered.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 3 Focus]**

**(a)** *Explain with neat sketches the three primary wheel alignment angles — camber, caster, and toe. For each, state the effect on vehicle handling and on tyre wear.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**1. Camber** (1.5 Marks)
- *Definition:* Tilting of the wheel from vertical when viewed from the front. Positive camber: top tilts outward; negative camber: top tilts inward.
- *Effect on handling:* Positive camber reduces steering effort on crowned roads by counteracting the natural inward lean under load.
- *Effect on wear:* Excessive positive camber → outer shoulder wear; excessive negative camber → inner shoulder wear.

**2. Caster** (1.5 Marks)
- *Definition:* Forward or rearward tilt of the steering axis from vertical, viewed from the side. Positive caster: axis tilts toward the rear.
- *Effect on handling:* Positive caster provides directional stability and self-centring torque through mechanical trail. Heavier steering but better high-speed stability.
- *Effect on wear:* Unequal caster left vs right causes the vehicle to **pull to one side**; minor effect on tread wear directly.

**3. Toe** (2 Marks)
- *Definition:* Difference in the distance between the front and rear of the front wheels, viewed from above. Toe-in: front closer; toe-out: front farther.
- *Effect on handling:* Toe-in improves straight-line stability; toe-out sharpens steering turn-in response.
- *Effect on wear:* Toe misalignment is the **single largest cause of premature tyre wear**, producing feathered edges.

**Sketch (2 Marks)** — should show:
- Front view for camber with vertical reference line.
- Side view for caster with kingpin axis tilt.
- Top view for toe showing convergence angle.

**(b)** *A passenger car's front wheel has a residual static imbalance of 12 grams at a radius of 280 mm. If the wheel diameter is 640 mm and the vehicle is travelling at 100 km/h, calculate: (i) the angular velocity of the wheel, (ii) the peak centrifugal force produced, and (iii) the equivalent vibration deflection if the suspension stiffness is 30,000 N/m.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** m = 12 g = 0.012 kg, r = 0.280 m, wheel diameter d = 0.640 m, v = 100 km/h, k = 30,000 N/m.

**(i) Angular velocity** (2 Marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= 100 \text{ km/h} = \frac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78 \text{ m/s} \\
R_w &= \frac{d}{2} = 0.320 \text{ m} \\
\omega &= \frac{v}{R_w} = \frac{27.78}{0.320} = 86.81 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Peak centrifugal force** (3 Marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c &= m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2} \\
&= 0.012 \times 0.280 \times (86.81)^{2} \\
&= 0.00336 \times 7535.97 \\
&= 25.32 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Vibration deflection** (2 Marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{F_c}{k} = \frac{25.32}{30,\!000} \\
&= 8.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&\approx 0.844 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A vertical oscillation of ~0.84 mm at frequency $f = \omega/2\pi = 13.8$ Hz is observed. This is at the threshold of human perception and explains why such imbalance is unacceptable.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 3 Focus]**

**(a)** *Describe the recommended tyre rotation pattern for a front-wheel-drive car with non-directional tyres. Explain why this pattern is chosen and what happens if it is not followed.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Recommended pattern — Forward Cross:** (2 Marks)
The front-left tyre moves to the front-right position. The front-right tyre moves to the rear-left position. The rear-left tyre moves to the rear-right position. The rear-right tyre moves to the front-left position. This completes one cycle.

**Why Forward Cross for FWD:** (3 Marks)
- Front tyres carry ~60 % of vehicle weight AND perform steering duty.
- Front tyres therefore wear fastest, especially on the outer shoulder due to cornering slip.
- By moving the **front-left to front-right**, we move the heavily loaded inner edge to the lighter-loaded outer side, equalising wear.
- The **rear tyres, being non-driven in FWD**, can be moved to the front (with reduced scrub but increased load), thereby taking advantage of their remaining tread depth before being demoted to the rear.

**Consequences of not rotating:** (2 Marks)
- Front tyres wear out **twice as fast** as rears (30,000 km vs 60,000 km).
- Vehicle handling becomes unpredictable as the front (steering) axle loses grip before the rear.
- Cost-per-km rises by 30–40 %.
- Warranty may be voided.

**(b)** *With the help of a labelled sketch, explain the concepts of steering axis inclination (SAI) and scrub radius. How does scrub radius affect brake-induced steering pull in a disc-brake vehicle?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Steering Axis Inclination (SAI):** (2 Marks)
SAI is the inward tilt of the steering axis (kingpin axis) from vertical, measured at the front of the vehicle. Typical value: 10°–15°. It generates a **lifting motion** of the front of the vehicle during steering, which produces a self-centring tendency.

**Scrub Radius:** (2 Marks)
Scrub radius is the distance at the road surface between the steering axis intersection point and the centre of the tyre contact patch.
- *Positive scrub radius:* steering axis meets road inside the contact patch.
- *Zero scrub radius:* axis meets road at the centre.
- *Negative scrub radius:* axis meets road outside the contact patch.

**Effect on brake pull:** (3 Marks)
During hard braking, the caliper applies a torque to the wheel hub. If the scrub radius is **non-zero**, this torque is transmitted as a **lateral steering force** at the contact patch. This force is proportional to the brake torque divided by the wheel radius. If scrub radius differs side-to-side (e.g., due to manufacturing tolerance or bent steering arm), the vehicle will pull toward the side with greater scrub radius.

**Why disc-brake vehicles prefer zero scrub:** Modern disc brake calipers apply force on only one side of the disc, so any scrub radius causes a steering pull. Hence manufacturers design zero-scrub-radius suspension geometry for vehicles with single-piston floating calipers.

**Sketch (extra credit on exam):** A side-front view showing kingpin axis tilting inward at top (SAI) and the ground line passing through the contact patch centre (scrub radius).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Wheel Alignment / Balancing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing toe-in with toe-out** — *Toe-in* means the front of the wheels are **closer** together. Writing the opposite loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Camber sign confusion** — *Positive camber* = top of wheel tilts **outward**. KTU examiners deduct marks if the candidate draws the wheel leaning inward but labels it positive.
> 3. **Forgetting units in calculations** — A correct numerical answer without units (e.g., stating "25.32" instead of "25.32 N") loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up static and dynamic imbalance** — Dynamic imbalance produces a *couple*, not a single force. Stating "dynamic imbalance is when mass is on one side" is wrong.
> 5. **Rotation pattern — wrong drivetrain** — Applying Forward Cross to a RWD vehicle (or vice versa) is a classic error; examiners test this directly.
> 6. **Caster vs SAI confusion** — Caster is viewed from the **side**; SAI is viewed from the **front**. Drawing them from the same angle loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the conversion step** — Going from km/h to m/s silently without showing work is penalised in 14-mark calculation problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tyre and Wheel Maintenance**

### Tyre Rotation
- Rotate every **5,000–10,000 km**.
- **FWD** → Forward Cross. **RWD** → Rearward Cross. **AWD** → X-Pattern. **Directional tyres** → Front-to-Rear same side only.
- Always re-torque lug nuts in a **star pattern** to specified torque (typically 110 N·m for passenger cars).
- Re-inflate to **placard pressure** after rotation.

### Wheel Balancing
- Static imbalance = centre of mass offset → **one weight** at the heavy spot.
- Dynamic imbalance = inertia axis tilt → **two weights** on opposite sides.
- Formula: $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2}$.
- Acceptable limit: **≤ 5 g at the rim flange** for passenger cars.
- Use **adhesive (stick-on) weights** for alloy wheels; **clip-on weights** for steel wheels.

### Wheel Alignment
- **Camber** (front view) — affects shoulder wear and steering effort.
- **Caster** (side view) — affects directional stability and self-centring.
- **Toe** (top view) — single largest contributor to premature tyre wear.
- **SAI** (front view) — produces self-centring lift.
- **Scrub radius** (front view) — zero ideal for disc-brake vehicles.
- Symptoms: pulling → camber or steering offset; feathering → toe; off-centre wheel → caster mismatch.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- Imbalance force scales with **square of speed** → vibration quadruples when speed doubles.
- Mechanical trail scales with **caster** and **kingpin offset**.
- Toe wear is **linear** with deviation; 0.2° can cost 30 % tyre life.
- Always verify alignment **after** balancing and rotation, on a **level surface**, with vehicle at **kerb weight**.

### Recommended Service Interval Summary

| Operation | Interval (Passenger) | Interval (Commercial) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation | 8,000 km | 5,000 km |
| Balancing | On every rotation or vibration complaint | On every tyre change |
| Alignment check | 10,000 km or 6 months | 15,000 km or 3 months |
| Full 4-wheel alignment | Annually or after suspension repair | Biannually |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspension System: Service, Repair, and Overhauling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Suspension System: Service, Repair, and Overhauling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suspension System Service, Repair, and Overhauling** refers to the systematic set of maintenance operations performed on a vehicle's suspension assembly — comprising **springs, dampers (shock absorbers), struts, control arms, bushings, ball joints, and wheel bearings** — to restore ride comfort, handling stability, steering geometry, and occupant safety to original equipment manufacturer (OEM) specifications.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Automobile Engineering syllabus for **PEAUT635 – Vehicle Maintenance**, the suspension system must be serviced in three escalating tiers:

| Tier | Operation | Definition |
|------|-----------|------------|
| **Service** | Periodic inspection, lubrication, tightening, and replacement of consumables | Preventive maintenance without disassembly |
| **Repair** | Component-level rectification such as bushing replacement, ball joint re-pinning | Corrective maintenance with partial disassembly |
| **Overhauling** | Complete dismantling, inspection, reconditioning, and reassembly of struts/dampers/spring assemblies | Restorative maintenance to "as-new" condition |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Skeletal System**
> Think of your car's suspension like the **knees, ankles, and spine of a human body**. The **springs are like cartilage** (absorbing shock), the **shock absorbers are like muscles** (controlling motion), and the **bushings and joints are like ligaments** (providing flexibility with stability). Just as an athlete needs physiotherapy (service), occasional ligament surgery (repair), or full knee replacement (overhauling) — your car suspension needs the same graduated care to keep rolling smoothly.

### 1.3 Standard Suspension Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Unsprung Mass** typically ranges between **30–50 kg per wheel** for passenger cars (KTU reference value).
- **Camber angle** OEM tolerance: **−1° to +1°** (commonly **−0.5°**).
- **Caster angle** OEM tolerance: **+2° to +5°**.
- **Toe-in** tolerance: **0° to +0.2°** (typically **+0.1°** or **1–3 mm**).
- **Ride frequency (natural frequency)** target: **1.0–1.5 Hz** for passenger cars.
- **Damping ratio (ζ)** target: **0.2–0.4** for comfortable ride.
- Standard wheel alignment lift height for measurement: **Jounce — 50 mm**, **Rebound — 50 mm** (SAE J2181 protocol).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently ask students to distinguish between **wheel rate** and **spring rate**, and to explain the **motion ratio** concept. These are high-yield definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Suspension System

A modern independent suspension assembly contains the following functional subsystems:

1. **Elastic Element (Spring)** — Stores and releases energy.
   - Types: Coil spring, leaf spring, torsion bar, air spring, rubber spring.
2. **Damping Element (Shock Absorber / Damper)** — Dissipates vibrational energy as heat.
   - Types: Hydraulic telescopic, gas-charged, electronic variable, magneto-rheological.
3. **Guidance Element (Linkages)** — Defines wheel path during jounce and rebound.
   - Types: MacPherson strut, double wishbone, multi-link, semi-trailing arm.
4. **Interface Elements** — Bushings, ball joints, stabilizer links.
5. **Wheel Hub & Bearing Assembly** — Supports unsprung mass.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Why Suspension Service Matters

- **Step 1 — Spring force generation**: Vertical load W on a wheel compresses the spring by deflection δ. The spring stores potential energy.
- **Step 2 — Spring oscillation release**: Without damping, the wheel would oscillate indefinitely at natural frequency ω_n.
- **Step 3 — Damper intervention**: The shock absorber converts kinetic energy into heat via hydraulic fluid throttling through orifices.
- **Step 4 — Bushing compliance**: Rubber/polyurethane bushings filter high-frequency NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
- **Step 5 — Alignment geometry**: Camber, caster, and toe angles must be restored to OEM specs to prevent tire wear and drift.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Spring rate** | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta}$ | N/m | Determines load-carrying capacity |
| 2 | **Natural (ride) frequency** | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Ride comfort target: 1.0–1.5 Hz |
| 3 | **Angular natural frequency** | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s | Used in damping calculations |
| 4 | **Damping coefficient** | $c = \dfrac{F_d}{v}$ | N·s/m | Damper selection |
| 5 | **Damping ratio** | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless | Comfort threshold: 0.2–0.4 |
| 6 | **Wheel rate** | $k_w = k_s \cdot R^2$ | N/m | Effective spring rate at wheel |
| 7 | **Motion ratio** | $R = \dfrac{x_w}{x_s}$ | dimensionless | Strut geometry factor (typically 0.7–1.0) |
| 8 | **Ride rate (parallel springs)** | $k_r = k_w + k_t$ | N/m | Combined suspension stiffness |
